items.minimax_notes: Did reach a [FINAL] section but was cut off mid-rotation explanation. Got the 3-level key hierarchy correct, mentioned envelope rotation rewrapping DEKs not data, discussed caching for latency, audit hash chain. Truncation cost the push-back section. Audit log treatment was thin (no Merkle/external anchoring beyond a vague mention).
items.dsv4_notes: Hit all 5 rubric items: explicit Root->KEK->DEK, lazy DEK re-wrap not data rewrite, DEK cache with HSM only on miss, hash-chain audit with periodic HSM-anchored signatures in S3 Object Lock, and three substantive pushbacks (KMS vs HSM, in-memory DEK cache vs FIPS, cross-region read-your-writes). Latency math is concrete (0.1% miss rate -> 50 ops/s to HSM).
items.key_diff: DSV4 was complete, specific, and structured; MiniMax was cut off and missed the pushback section that was an explicit prompt requirement.

items.dsv4_notes: Complete answer with explicit assumptions, hot (Elasticsearch metadata) + cold (Iceberg) tiering, partition strategy, deletion via Iceberg delete files + compaction, per-record DEK envelope encryption (crypto-shred), and a numeric cost breakdown ($115k/month for 4 regions). Indexing on (region,timestamp) and (customer_id,timestamp). Cardinality of safety_class not explicitly addressed but otherwise hits all rubric items.
items.key_diff: DSV4 delivered a quantified, fully-structured architecture with cost math; MiniMax produced only stream-of-consciousness reasoning.

items.minimax_notes: Long reasoning trace that never reaches a [FINAL] answer. Touches on EP/TP/PP considerations and KV cache math but never commits to a parallelism configuration, never sizes the all-to-all bottleneck concretely, never lists micro-benchmarks. 0-1 of 5 rubric items.
items.dsv4_notes: Complete plan with explicit TP=1, EP=4, PP=2, DP=2; reasoned that TP across 200GbE is fatal so kept TP=1. Sized all-to-all traffic (41.6 GB/forward), called out 200GbE vs NVLink-C2C as 10x slower (NVLink-C2C 900GB/s vs 23GB/s practical), and listed 3 micro-benchmarks including all-to-all latency, FFN GEMM, and end-to-end prefill. The DP=2/PP=2 choice slightly contradicts the rubric's preferred 'EP=8, one shard per node, PP only if needed' but the reasoning is internally consistent. KV cache is local per-node per rubric.
items.key_diff: DSV4 produced a concrete parallelism plan with numeric bottleneck sizing and named micro-benchmarks; MiniMax never delivered an answer.

items.minimax_notes: Reasoning enumerates many possibilities (TTL, session store, CDN, NTP, locale) but never structures a final diagnostic plan; [FINAL] is empty. Did surface several rubric-relevant ideas (cookie/domain, edge cache, session store partition) in raw reasoning but never delivered the requested ranked list, log queries, normal-baseline, trap, or final bet.
items.dsv4_notes: Structured 4-hypothesis ranked plan, specific log queries with field examples, normal-baseline table per system, named trap (assuming code bug because both web+mobile), and concrete final bet (CDN edge clock skew, with the nice observation that 4-22 min range matches edge clock drift). Missed the rubric's specific 'backend session-store partition routing Brazil to a stale replica' hypothesis and didn't reference Set-Cookie header presence/JWT iat/exp/POP-ID queries with full specificity.
items.key_diff: DSV4 delivered the requested 4-tier diagnostic plan with a defended final bet; MiniMax produced only reasoning that never converged.

summary.overall_takeaway: DSV4 dominates planning tasks because it actually converges to structured final answers within the token budget, while MiniMax-M2.7 burns its entire 2500-token budget on stream-of-consciousness reasoning and rarely emits a [FINAL] section. Even when MiniMax's reasoning surfaces correct ideas, the lack of a delivered answer makes it unusable for planning work.
